claims.claim: We investigate whether a lightweight student model, given only insertion-time token and key features, can predict the future saliency of key-value (KV) cache entries well enough to inform cache eviction decisions.

evidence_refs.quote: # Predicting Future KV-Cache Saliency at Insertion Time: A Student Policy Approach

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a controlled synthetic attention simulator, a histogram-gradient-boosting student policy retained a mean of 46.6% (SD 0.21%) of future attention mass at a 20% KV budget across three random seeds, compared to 5.4% for recency eviction and 20.1% for random eviction; the oracle upper bound was 47.2%.

claims.claim: Top-20 saliency classification ROC-AUC averaged 0.991 and Spearman rank correlation averaged 0.966 across seeds.

claims.claim: A supplementary smoke test on a tiny GPT-2 model showed near-perfect student recovery of oracle saliency, but this result is not scientifically decisive because the model's attention patterns appear to be almost fully determined by positional features.

claims.claim: Let $s_t = \sum_{u=t+1}^{T} \alpha_{u,t}$ denote the *future saliency* of position $t$, where $\alpha_{u,t}$ is the attention weight from query position $u$ to key position $t$.

claims.claim: Formally, if $\mathcal{R}$ is the set of retained positions: $$\text{retention} = \frac{\sum_{t \in \mathcal{R}} s_t}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} s_t}$$ A student is useful if it materially outperforms recency and random baselines and approaches oracle retention.
